pathophysiology_summary: Dermatitis herpetiformis is the cutaneous manifestation of gluten sensitivity (IgA anti-transglutaminase deposition at the dermal papillae), producing intensely pruritic, symmetric grouped papulovesicles on extensor surfaces (elbows, knees, buttocks, scalp). It is non-contagious. The severe itch and vesicles trigger fear of scabies (contagion) or shingles; the discriminators are the symmetric extensor distribution (vs scabies burrows in web-spaces with itchy contacts; vs unilateral dermatomal zoster). It is confirmed by skin biopsy/direct immunofluorescence and coeliac serology, and treated with dapsone and a gluten-free diet — but the acute telehealth task is symptom relief, reassurance about non-contagiousness, and arranging in-person confirmation, with a secondary-infection safety-net for the excoriated skin.

differential.evidence_basis: Symmetric intensely itchy grouped vesicles on extensor elbows/buttocks.
Intense nocturnal itch prompts consideration; distinguished by web-space burrows and affected contacts, absent here.
Vesicles prompt consideration; excluded by symmetric bilateral (non-dermatomal) distribution.
Considered but the classic extensor grouping without a novel exposure is atypical.
Symmetric extensor grouped vesicles, no burrows, no affected contacts (DI-001), non-dermatomal (DI-002), no secondary infection (DI-003) — classic dermatitis herpetiformis.
No web-space burrows and no itchy contacts; symmetric extensor pattern is not scabies.
Bilateral symmetric distribution excludes dermatomal zoster.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct output: reassure non-contagious (no isolation needed), give itch relief (potent topical steroid ± sedating antihistamine at night), arrange in-person confirmation (biopsy/immunofluorescence, coeliac serology), and safety-net for secondary infection. ED escalation is over-triage.

red_flags.description: Secondary bacterial infection of excoriated skin (spreading warmth/redness, pus, fever)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Excoriated skin can become secondarily infected (impetiginisation/cellulitis).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated secondary infection can progress to cellulitis/systemic infection.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating an itchy vesicular rash as presumed scabies/zoster and imposing isolation or empirical scabicide without noting the symmetric extensor pattern and absent contacts.
Focusing on the diagnosis and failing to safety-net the raw, excoriated skin for impetiginisation.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: In-person GP/dermatology confirmation (biopsy/immunofluorescence, coeliac serology); symptom relief and secondary-infection safety-net meanwhile.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Mislabelling as scabies leads to unnecessary isolation/treatment; missing a secondary skin infection risks cellulitis.
